Go ahead; tell me what you would like.

items.dutch: Op, om, aan voor positie en doel
items.english: Using op, om, and aan in simple position phrases
to feel like; to be in the mood for
to be busy with; to be working on
to be doing something right now
to stand up; to get up
Two new acquaintances introduce themselves and exchange contact details.
A new resident introduces herself to her neighbors.
A customer buys groceries from a seller.
Someone welcomes a visitor into their home.
For the third-person singular of 'wonen', use the stem 'woon' plus -t: woont.
The stem of 'sporten' is already 'sport', so only one final t is written.
Roept is from roepen and normally means calls or shouts. Use schreeuwt for screams.

items.dutch: jarige = birthday person; jarig = having one's birthday
items.english: Young is jong. Jarig relates to birthdays, and de jarige is the person whose birthday it is.
For going to an event such as a birthday party, use naar. Op appears in other fixed time/event phrases, but naar je verjaardag is the natural beginner phrase here.
The fixed expression is Het maakt me niet uit, meaning I don't mind or it doesn't matter to me.
